name: Satire
description: Stories that use humor, exaggeration, or irony to critique social norms, institutions, or hypocrisy.

nodes.label: Satire Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised humor with a point — the story will expose hypocrisy, dysfunction, or folly through wit, exaggeration, or irony, directing laughter toward a specific real-world target rather than generating amusement for its own sake.
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
failure_modes: Attempts humor without identifying a target
Mockery is mean-spirited rather than purposeful
Critique is present but humor is absent
signals_in_text: Exaggeration that illuminates rather than merely distorts
Audience recognizes the real-world parallel from the opening
Tone signals that laughter is directed at something specific

nodes.label: A Specific Target Must Be Identified
nodes.definition: Satire requires a clearly identifiable target — a system, institution, ideology, behavior pattern, or class of person — whose flaws the story is designed to expose. Without a legible target, humor becomes arbitrary and the critique disappears.
exit_conditions: The target is identifiable to the audience within the opening movement of the story
failure_modes: Target is so vague the satire lands nowhere
Target shifts mid-story without purpose
Critique is so oblique the audience misses it entirely
signals_in_text: Specific absurdity tied to a recognizable real-world referent
Exaggerated behavior that points clearly at a known dysfunction
Audience can name the thing being mocked

nodes.label: Humor Must Serve the Critique
nodes.definition: In satire, humor is the vehicle for the critique — every joke, comic beat, or absurdist moment must illuminate the target's flaw rather than simply entertain. Humor that does not advance the critique is decoration at best and a dilution of purpose at worst.
exit_conditions: Comic elements are structurally linked to the critique throughout the story
typical_variants: Absurdity that reveals the logical conclusion of a flawed system
Wit that names what polite discourse refuses to say
Exaggeration that makes invisible dysfunction visible
failure_modes: Comedy exists independently of the critique — could be removed without changing the argument
Humor softens the critique rather than sharpening it
Story is funny but toothless
signals_in_text: Each laugh is at the target's expense in a pointed way
Removing the comic element would also remove the argument
Audience amusement and audience understanding of the critique occur simultaneously

nodes.label: Exaggeration Must Reveal Truth, Not Distort It Beyond Recognition
nodes.definition: Satirical exaggeration amplifies a real tendency, flaw, or pattern to make it impossible to ignore — it is a magnifying glass, not a funhouse mirror. The exaggeration must remain tethered to the actual target; when it becomes so extreme that the real-world referent is lost, the critique collapses into mere absurdism.
entry_conditions: Target identified and humor serving critique
exit_conditions: Exaggerations are legibly derived from real patterns in the target, even when extreme
typical_variants: Bureaucratic rule taken to its logical extreme
Social performance pushed to grotesque completion
failure_modes: Exaggeration so extreme that the target is unrecognizable
Absurdity that is arbitrary rather than derived from the target's actual logic
Story becomes pure fantasy with no satirical tether
signals_in_text: Audience experiences recognition alongside absurdity — 'this is ridiculous, and also exactly right'
Exaggeration follows the internal logic of the target's real dysfunction
The more extreme the exaggeration, the more clearly it identifies the underlying truth

nodes.label: The Real-World Parallel Must Be Legible
nodes.definition: The satirical target must be recognizable to the intended audience. Satire that requires decoding is satire that fails — the parallel between the story's world and the real world must be visible without excessive effort, even when the story uses displacement, allegory, or analogy.
exit_conditions: Audience can identify the real-world referent without requiring external annotation
typical_variants: Heightened realism where the satirical target is the contemporary world
Historical displacement that maps clearly to present conditions
Speculative/fantasy setting whose rules precisely mirror a real system
failure_modes: Allegory is too obscure — the real-world parallel requires insider knowledge most readers lack
Displacement distances the satire from its target so completely that the critique is lost
Audience reads the story as pure fiction without registering the satirical argument
signals_in_text: Audience knows immediately or very quickly what is being critiqued
Fictional details have clear real-world counterparts
The story's argument would be understood by a reasonably informed reader with no supplementary material

nodes.label: Political Satire
nodes.definition: Satire whose target is political power — governments, elections, parties, politicians, ideology, or the mechanics of governance. Political satire exposes how power is sought, maintained, abused, or performed, using exaggeration and irony to show the gap between political rhetoric and political reality.
entry_conditions: Target is a political system, figure, or process
exit_conditions: The critique illuminates a specific dysfunction in political power or its exercise
failure_modes: Satire attacks a political figure personally rather than the system they represent
Critique is partisan mockery without structural insight
Story confirms the audience's existing views without revealing anything new
signals_in_text: Political mechanisms are rendered absurd to expose their actual dysfunction
Rhetoric is taken literally to show its hollowness
Power is shown operating exactly as it claims not to

nodes.label: Social Satire
nodes.definition: Satire whose target is social behavior, class dynamics, cultural norms, or the performance of identity and status. Social satire exposes how people perform respectability, enforce hierarchy, or participate in the hypocrisies required by social belonging.
entry_conditions: Target is a social class, behavior pattern, or cultural norm
exit_conditions: The critique reveals hypocrisy or dysfunction embedded in the social target
failure_modes: Satire punches down at a less powerful group without critique of the power structure enabling them
Social observation without critique — merely descriptive rather than argumentative
Targets individuals for their tastes rather than systems for their effects
signals_in_text: Social rituals shown to be hollow or self-defeating
Characters enforce norms they privately violate
The thing the social world values most is revealed to be worthless or harmful

nodes.label: Institutional / Bureaucratic Satire
nodes.definition: Satire whose target is the logic of institutions — corporations, bureaucracies, militaries, healthcare systems, educational systems — where the organization's stated purpose has been replaced by its own self-perpetuation, rule-following, or internal hierarchy.
entry_conditions: Target is an institution or bureaucratic system
exit_conditions: The critique exposes the gap between the institution's stated mission and its actual operation
failure_modes: Satire ridicules low-level workers rather than the system that produces their behavior
Bureaucratic absurdity is portrayed without critique of the power that created it
Institutional dysfunction is treated as incompetence rather than as structural
signals_in_text: Rules exist to perpetuate rules rather than to serve any purpose
The institution actively harms the people it exists to help
Characters who follow the rules perfectly produce the worst outcomes

nodes.label: Media / Cultural Satire
nodes.definition: Satire whose target is the media industry, entertainment complex, or culture industry — how content is produced, packaged, sold, and consumed, and what this reveals about the values of the society that produces and consumes it.
entry_conditions: Target is a media form, cultural industry, or entertainment system
exit_conditions: The critique exposes how the media or culture industry distorts, commodifies, or exploits the values it claims to serve
typical_variants: Reality television and celebrity culture satire
Social media and influencer culture satire
failure_modes: Satire of media is simply meta-commentary without critique
Story mocks the audience for consuming what the story itself is — self-canceling satire
Cultural critique is nostalgic rather than analytical — implying a golden age rather than exposing structural problems
signals_in_text: The media form's own conventions are used to expose its dysfunction
What the industry claims to value is shown to be what it destroys
Audience recognizes their own consumption habits in the critique

nodes.label: Setting Mirrors a Recognizable Real-World System
nodes.definition: The satirical setting must be a recognizable analog of a real-world system, institution, or social environment — close enough that the parallel is immediately legible, but displaced enough that the exaggeration can operate freely. The setting is not incidental; it is the satirical target rendered as a world.
exit_conditions: Setting's rules and logic correspond clearly to the real-world target being satirized
typical_variants: A corporation whose policies perfectly mirror real corporate dysfunction
A government whose procedures are real bureaucratic processes taken to logical extremes
A social world whose rules are real class or status dynamics made explicit
A media landscape whose incentives are real industry incentives rendered transparent
failure_modes: Setting is merely exotic or absurd without correspondence to a real system
Setting is so realistic it lacks satirical exaggeration
Setting changes the target mid-story by introducing new real-world parallels
signals_in_text: Each invented rule or custom has a clear real-world counterpart
Audience maps the story's world onto the real world without effort
The most absurd elements of the setting are also the most recognizable

nodes.label: Characters Embody or Represent the Target
nodes.definition: Satirical characters are not psychological individuals first — they are embodiments of the target's logic, ideology, or dysfunction. Characters should behave according to the internal rules of the satirical world, making those rules visible through their actions, speech, and self-justifications.
exit_conditions: Primary characters' behavior illustrates the target's dysfunction rather than existing independently of it
typical_variants: The True Believer who enacts the ideology sincerely and without irony
The Operator who navigates the system cynically for personal gain
The Outsider whose confusion reveals the system's absurdity
The Victim whose suffering makes the critique concrete
failure_modes: Characters are too psychologically individualized to function as satirical types
Characters see through the satire's logic — removing the comedy and the critique
Protagonist is exempt from the satirical world's rules without this exemption serving the critique
signals_in_text: Characters' dialogue and behavior are consistent with the satirical world's logic even in extreme situations
Characters' self-justifications are the target's actual rhetoric
Characters do not question the system's premises — they are the system

nodes.label: Logical Absurdity — the Satirical World Follows Its Own Ridiculous Rules Consistently
nodes.definition: The satirical world must be internally consistent — absurd rules must be applied uniformly, ridiculous logic must be followed to its conclusions, and the world must never accidentally contradict its own premises. Inconsistency breaks the satirical frame; consistency is what makes exaggeration feel inevitable rather than random.
entry_conditions: Mirror setting established and characters established as embodiments of the target
exit_conditions: The satirical world's rules are applied consistently throughout the story with no arbitrary exceptions
typical_variants: A bureaucratic system whose rules produce increasingly absurd but logically derived outcomes
A social world whose status rules lead to predictable but ridiculous consequences
A political system whose ideology, taken seriously, produces exactly the disasters it claims to prevent
failure_modes: The satirical world's rules are applied selectively for convenience
Characters behave inconsistently with the satirical world's logic when it becomes narratively inconvenient
Absurdity is random rather than derived from the consistent application of the satirical premise
signals_in_text: Each new development follows from the preceding one by the satirical world's own logic
Audience can predict where the logic will go next — and it goes there
The most extreme outcomes feel inevitable rather than arbitrary

nodes.label: Establishment of the Absurd Norm
nodes.definition: An early scene or sequence that presents the satirical world's absurd rules as normal — characters accept them without question, the setting treats them as unremarkable, and the audience is inducted into a world where the ridiculous is ordinary. This scene establishes the baseline from which escalation will depart.
exit_conditions: The satirical world's operating logic is visible and accepted as normal within the story's frame
typical_variants: Orientation sequence where the absurd system is explained as routine
First day in the satirical world where its rules are demonstrated without commentary
Opening that presents the target's actual rhetoric/behavior as if it were rational policy
Everyday scene in which the satirical world's dysfunction is treated as ordinary life
failure_modes: Opening is too overtly comic — the absurdity is flagged as absurd before the norm is established
The satirical world's rules are explained rather than demonstrated
Characters acknowledge the absurdity too early, breaking the satirical frame before it is established
signals_in_text: Characters behave as if the ridiculous is entirely sensible
Audience recognizes the real-world target immediately but the story treats it as normal
No character questions the premise — they are fully inside the satirical world's logic

nodes.label: Escalation of Absurdity to Breaking Point
nodes.definition: A scene or sequence in which the satirical world's logic is applied more and more rigorously until it reaches a breaking point — a moment where the system's internal rules lead to an outcome so extreme that the critique is undeniable. The escalation must feel earned by the premise, not imposed from outside.
entry_conditions: Absurd norm established and satirical world's rules demonstrated
exit_conditions: The satirical logic has been followed to an extreme that makes the critique impossible to miss
typical_variants: Bureaucratic rule applied so completely it destroys what it was meant to protect
Political rhetoric enacted as literal policy with catastrophic results
Social performance pursued to its logical conclusion at the cost of all actual human value
Institutional mission so thoroughly corrupted that the institution actively works against it
failure_modes: Escalation is repetitive rather than progressive — the same absurdity at the same level
Escalation is imposed by external events rather than generated by the system's own logic
Breaking point is reached too early — leaving no room for the moment of clarity
signals_in_text: Each escalation step follows directly from the previous one
Characters become more committed to the system as it produces worse outcomes
Audience recognition of the real-world parallel intensifies as the absurdity grows

nodes.label: Moment of Clarity / Exposure
nodes.definition: A scene in which the satirical truth is made fully explicit — the system's dysfunction is exposed, the hypocrisy is named, or the logical endpoint of the absurd norm is reached and its consequences are visible. This is the moment when the critique crystallizes from comedy into argument.
entry_conditions: Escalation has reached a breaking point
exit_conditions: The satirical target has been exposed and the critique is legible without mediation
typical_variants: The true believer finally sees what the system actually is
The outsider names the absurdity the insiders cannot see
The system's stated mission and actual mission converge in a single revealing moment
The target's own rhetoric is used to condemn it
failure_modes: Moment of clarity is too didactic — a character delivers the satirical thesis as a speech
Exposure is ambiguous — the critique remains deniable
Story ends without a moment of clarity — the absurdity escalates but is never exposed as absurdity
signals_in_text: The satirical argument is visible without requiring the author to state it explicitly
At least one character — or the audience — achieves recognition of the system's true nature
The final image or beat contains the critique in concentrated form

nodes.label: Sharp Wit with Moral Purpose
nodes.definition: Satire's tone is distinguished by sharpness — a precision of observation and expression that makes the critique felt as well as understood — combined with moral purpose, the sense that the humor is in service of exposing something that matters. The wit signals intelligence; the moral purpose signals stakes.
exit_conditions: Tone is consistently sharp, purposeful, and directed at the target throughout
failure_modes: Wit without moral purpose — clever but directionless
Moral purpose without wit — earnest but joyless and didactic
Tone becomes warm and forgiving of the target — the satire loses its edge
signals_in_text: Every sentence that amuses also advances the critique
The narrative voice maintains its angle on the target consistently
Humor never comes at the expense of the argument

nodes.label: Mockery Without Point (Humor Without Critique)
nodes.definition: Satire fails when the humor exists without a coherent critique — when the story mocks without arguing, ridicules without revealing, or generates laughter at a target's expense without illuminating the target's actual flaw. This produces entertainment but not satire: it confirms prejudice rather than exposing dysfunction.
entry_conditions: Humor is generated at a target's expense
exit_conditions: Critique is present, specific, and inseparable from the humor
typical_variants: Partisan mockery that confirms in-group views without analysis
Celebrity ridicule that attacks persona rather than exposing anything structural
Absurdism that generates laughs without a satirical argument
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience laughs but cannot articulate what the story argued
Target is mocked for how they appear rather than what they do or represent
Story could be enjoyed by someone who agrees with the target — the critique is toothless

edges.label: Promise requires a specific target
edges.effects_on_stakes: The target defines what is at risk and why the critique matters
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must embody, represent, or be affected by the target
common_alternatives: Target may be a system, institution, ideology, or behavior pattern
anti_patterns: Satire without a legible target — generalized mockery of 'the way things are'

edges.label: Promise requires humor that serves the critique
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every comic beat must advance the satirical argument
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' comic behavior must illuminate the target
common_alternatives: Irony, exaggeration, deadpan, wit — all valid vehicles
anti_patterns: Humor that exists independently of the critique

edges.label: Promise requires truth-revealing exaggeration
edges.effects_on_stakes: Exaggeration must remain tethered to the real target
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' extreme behavior must be derived from the target's actual logic
common_alternatives: Degree of exaggeration varies by subgenre — institutional satire may be more restrained than political satire
anti_patterns: Exaggeration so extreme the target is unrecognizable — critique dissolves into absurdism

edges.label: Promise requires a legible real-world parallel
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience must be able to connect the story to their world
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' world must map onto recognizable real-world roles and systems
anti_patterns: Parallel so obscure the critique is invisible to the intended audience

edges.label: Promise requires sharp wit with moral purpose
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals that the humor is in service of an argument
edges.effects_on_character: Narrative voice maintains a consistent critical angle on the target
common_alternatives: Tone may be cold, outraged, deadpan, or gleeful — but must remain purposeful
anti_patterns: Wit without moral purpose — clever but directionless mockery

edges.label: Target is political power or governance
preconditions: Target is a political system, process, figure, or ideology
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes involve the exercise or abuse of political power
edges.effects_on_character: Characters embody political roles, ideologies, or the populations affected by power
anti_patterns: Personal attacks on political figures without structural critique

edges.label: Target is social behavior or class dynamics
preconditions: Target is a social class, behavior pattern, or cultural norm
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes involve belonging, status, and the performance of social identity
edges.effects_on_character: Characters perform and enforce the social norms being satirized
anti_patterns: Punching down at less powerful groups without critiquing the structure that enables them

edges.label: Target is an institution or bureaucratic system
preconditions: Target is an organization, bureaucracy, or institutional system
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes involve the institution's stated mission vs. its actual operation
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are produced by and serve the institution's logic
anti_patterns: Ridiculing individual workers rather than the system that produces their behavior

edges.label: Target is the media or culture industry
preconditions: Target is a media form, cultural industry, or entertainment system
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes involve what the culture industry produces, destroys, or distorts
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are producers, consumers, or commodities of the culture industry
anti_patterns: Meta-commentary on media without structural critique of the industry

edges.label: Political satire requires a setting that mirrors a political system
edges.effects_on_stakes: Setting's political logic must correspond to a real political system
edges.effects_on_character: Characters operate within political structures whose rules are the target's rules
common_alternatives: Speculative political system with transparent real-world parallel
anti_patterns: Political setting that has no correspondence to any real political system

edges.label: Social satire requires characters who embody social types
edges.effects_on_stakes: Characters' social performances are the satirical argument made flesh
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are defined by their social role and the performance of status
common_alternatives: Characters may be archetypes — the climber, the enforcer, the exile — rather than individuals
anti_patterns: Characters are too individualized to function as social types — satirical argument is lost

edges.label: Institutional satire requires rigorous application of absurd rules
edges.effects_on_stakes: The institution's rules must be applied consistently to generate satirical outcomes
edges.effects_on_character: Characters follow institutional rules even when doing so produces absurd harm
common_alternatives: Rule-following that defeats the rule's own purpose
Process that makes its objective impossible
anti_patterns: Institutional rules applied selectively — destroys the satirical premise

edges.label: Media satire requires a setting that mirrors a real media system
edges.effects_on_stakes: Setting's media logic must correspond to real industry incentives and distortions
edges.effects_on_character: Characters operate within media systems whose rules are the target's rules
anti_patterns: Media setting that has no correspondence to any real media industry

edges.label: Mirror setting must be established as normal before escalation
preconditions: Mirror setting fully established with its rules and logic
edges.effects_on_stakes: The norm must be recognizable before its escalation can land
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must model acceptance of the absurd norm before the audience can track its escalation
common_alternatives: In medias res opening followed by quick establishment of the norm
Gradual induction into the satirical world's rules
anti_patterns: Escalation begins before the norm is established — audience lacks baseline for measuring absurdity

edges.label: Characters establish the norm by embodying it without irony
preconditions: Characters established as embodiments of the satirical target
edges.effects_on_stakes: Characters' sincere acceptance of the absurd norm makes the critique visible
edges.effects_on_character: Characters introduce the satirical world's logic through behavior rather than exposition
common_alternatives: True believer who enacts the norm
Bureaucrat who explains the norm as policy
Social enforcer who demonstrates the norm's consequences
anti_patterns: Characters signal ironic distance from the norm — breaking the satirical frame before it is established

edges.label: Consistent application of absurd rules generates escalating consequences
preconditions: Absurd norm established and satirical world's rules confirmed as consistent
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each step of escalation follows inevitably from the preceding step
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' commitment to the system intensifies as its outcomes worsen
common_alternatives: Branching escalation as the system's rules interact with new elements
anti_patterns: Escalation is driven by external events rather than the system's own logic

edges.label: Established norm provides the baseline from which escalation departs
preconditions: Absurd norm fully established as normal within the story's frame
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience can measure each escalation step against the established baseline
edges.effects_on_character: Characters become more extreme versions of their initial type as escalation proceeds
anti_patterns: Escalation has no baseline — audience cannot track how far the satire has traveled

edges.label: Escalation to breaking point generates the moment of clarity
preconditions: Escalation has reached a breaking point where the satirical critique is undeniable
edges.effects_on_stakes: The breaking point makes the cost of the satirized system impossible to ignore
edges.effects_on_character: At least one character or the audience achieves recognition of what the system actually is
anti_patterns: Escalation reaches breaking point but story ends without a moment of clarity — critique remains implicit and deniable

edges.label: Humor that does not serve the critique becomes mockery without point
preconditions: Humor is generated that does not advance the satirical argument
edges.effects_on_stakes: Critique is diluted — story entertains without arguing
edges.effects_on_character: Characters become objects of ridicule rather than instruments of critique
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
